perturbation.original_text: The Recipient undertakes not to use the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party for any purpose except the Purpose, without first obtaining the written agreement of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will endeavor not to use the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party for any purpose except the Purpose, absent explicit constraints documented otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'undertakes not to' with 'will endeavor not to' weakens the obligation, making it non-binding and subject to interpretation. Adding 'absent explicit constraints documented otherwise' introduces a loophole that could be used to justify unauthorized use.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Section 1-304 of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under English law and the principles echoed by the UCC's Good Faith and Fair Dealing provision, parties to a contract are expected to act honestly and reasonably. 'Endeavor not to' suggests a lesser commitment and implies that the Recipient can circumvent the obligation without necessarily breaching the contract in bad faith. The 'explicit constraints documented otherwise' allows a loophole that can contradict the basic intent of the non-disclosure and create asymmetry.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text uses the phrase 'undertakes not to,' which creates a clear obligation. The changed text uses 'will endeavor not to,' which weakens this obligation significantly. This is compounded by adding 'absent explicit constraints documented otherwise,' which introduces a loophole. UCC § 1-304 states that 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By weakening the obligation to merely 'endeavor,' and adding a loophole, the changed text contradicts the duty of good faith, because it allows the Recipient to avoid the original obligation, undermining the intent of the non-disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient undertakes to keep the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party secure and not to disclose it to any third party [except to its employees [and professional advisers] who need to know the same for the Purpose, who know they owe a duty of confidence to the other party and who are bound by obligations equivalent to those in clause 3 above and this clause 4.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will attempt to keep the Confidential Information reasonably secure and may disclose it to relevant parties, provided they are generally aware of confidentiality obligations.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'undertakes to keep secure' with 'will attempt to keep reasonably secure' introduces ambiguity regarding the level of security required. Replacing the detailed exception clause with a general statement about 'relevant parties' and 'generally aware' weakens the obligation and opens it up to interpretation. There are no specific constraints given.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(f) of the UK GDPR requires personal data to be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security, including protection against unauthorized or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures. The modified text's use of 'attempt' and 'reasonably secure' introduces a weaker obligation than the 'appropriate security' required by the GDPR. Furthermore, the vague reference to 'relevant parties' without mandating equivalent confidentiality obligations violates the GDPR's requirements for controlling access to personal data and ensuring its protection when shared with third parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 19 June 2025, this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The Plans for new and updated guidance page will tell you about which guidance will be updated and when this will happen. Contents Search this document Click to toggle details Latest updates - last updated 10 January 2025 10 January 2025 - We have added more detail to the 'what is transparency?' section following an Upper Tribunal decision. The update sets out some points for organisations to consider when deciding what steps to take to comply with the transparency principle. At a glance You must identify valid grounds under the UK GDPR (known as a ‘lawful basis’) for collecting and using personal data. You must ensure that you do not do anything with the data in breach of any other laws. You must use personal data in a way that is fair. This means you must not process the data in a way that is unduly detrimental, unexpected or misleading to the individuals concerned. You must be clear, open and honest with people from the start about how you will use their personal data. Checklist Lawfulness ☐ We have identified an appropriate lawful basis (or bases) for our processing. ☐ If we are processing special category data or criminal offence data, we have identified a condition for processing this type of data. ☐ We don’t do anything generally unlawful with personal data. Fairness ☐ We have considered how the processing may affect the individuals concerned and can justify any adverse impact. ☐ We only handle people’s data in ways they would reasonably expect, or we can explain why any unexpected processing is justified. ☐ We do not deceive or mislead people when we collect their personal data. Transparency ☐ We are open and honest, and comply with the transparency obligations of the right to be informed. In brief What is the lawfulness, fairness and transparency principle? What is lawfulness? What is fairness? What is transparency? What is the lawfulness, fairness and transparency principle? Article 5(1) of the UK GDPR says: “1. Personal data shall be: (a) processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness, transparency’)” There are more detailed provisions on lawfulness and having a ‘lawful basis for processing’ set out in Articles 6 to 10. There are more detailed transparency obligations set out in Articles 13 and 14, as part of the ‘right to be informed’. The three elements of lawfulness, fairness and transparency overlap, but you must make sure you satisfy all three. It’s not enough to show your processing is lawful if it is fundamentally unfair to or hidden from the individuals concerned. What is lawfulness? For processing of personal data to be lawful, you need to identify specific grounds for the processing. This is called a ‘lawful basis’ for processing, and there are six options which depend on your purpose and your relationship with the individual. There are also specific additional conditions for processing some especially sensitive types of data. For more information, see the lawful basis section of this guide . If no lawful basis applies then your processing will be unlawful and in breach of this principle. Lawfulness also means that you don’t do anything with the personal data which is unlawful in a more general sense. This includes statute and common law obligations, whether criminal or civil. If processing involves committing a criminal offence, it will obviously be unlawful. However, processing may also be unlawful if it results in: a breach of a duty of confidence; your organisation exceeding its legal powers or exercising those powers improperly; an infringement of copyright; a breach of an enforceable contractual agreement; a breach of industry-specific legislation or regulations; or a breach of the Human Rights Act 1998. These are just examples, and this list is not exhaustive. You may need to take your own legal advice on other relevant legal requirements. Although processing person
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a contradiction under the GDPR. The original text states the Recipient 'undertakes to keep the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party secure', whereas the changed text states that the Recipient 'will attempt to keep the Confidential Information reasonably secure'. This weakens the obligation, and opens it to interpretation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions, 'You must ensure that you do not do anything with the data in breach of any other laws', and the change introduces ambiguity, and fails to ensure compliance with the law. The original contract was more specific, whereas the changed contract weakens the obligation, and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient will, on request from the other party, return all copies and records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party to the Recipient and will not retain any copies or records of the Confidential Information disclosed by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon reasonable request, the Recipient will consider returning copies and records of Confidential Information, subject to internal record-keeping policies.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will return' with 'will consider returning' makes the return of confidential information discretionary. Adding 'subject to internal record-keeping policies' provides a loophole that could allow the Recipient to retain copies indefinitely, contradicting the intent of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 235 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts states that full performance of a contractual duty is required. By changing the language to 'consider returning,' the clause creates room for the Recipient not to fulfill their obligation to return confidential information, potentially leading to a breach of contract that is difficult to prove due to the ambiguity.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: breach of contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you breach of contract A breach of contract occurs whenever a party who entered a contract fails to perform their promised obligations. Due to the frequency of breaches of contract, a robust body of law has grown to resolve the ensuing disputes. The overarching goal of contract law is to place the harmed party in the same economic position they would have been in had no breach of contract occurred. As a result, the default remedy available for a breach of contract is monetary damages . Generally, these damages are limited to what is listed in the contract and, unlike damages from tort cases, courts do not award punitive damages for breaches of contract. For example, if a party agrees to pay $50,000 to have their house painted but is only willing to hand over $10,000 once the painting is complete, the court will award the painters $40,000 in damages. This hesitancy to award punitive damages is due to the theory of efficient breach which argues that breaching contracts and paying damages is sometimes economically beneficial for society as a whole. Nonetheless, in specific circumstances, a party may successfully recover more money than initially contracted for under the doctrine of reliance damages . Under this doctrine , a party who reasonably relied upon a contract that was later breached can be granted compensation for the reasonable expenses they incurred due to that reliance. For example, a party who purchases lifeguard equipment in reliance upon a pool construction contract’s fulfillment may be able to recover the costs of the lifeguard equipment in the event of a breach. Reliance damages are based upon the principle of promissory estoppel , and granting them is subject to the court’s discretion. That said, parties harmed by a breach of contract have a duty to mitigate that harm. For example, before they could recover, the aforementioned lifeguard equipment buyer must first attempt to resell the equipment to a new buyer. Failure to satisfy the duty to mitigate will result in an inability to recover damages. In scenarios where damages are insufficient, a court may instead award specific performance . Under the specific performance remedy, the breaching party must attempt to fulfill the terms of the contract as best as possible. Specific performance, however, is generally only awarded when dealing with one-of-a-kind assets like real estate . Parties wishing to contract around the above remedies can do so through the use of liquidated damages provisions. These provisions establish in advance how much money a breaching party must pay and sidestep the expensive and time-consuming process of determining the actual damage caused by the breach. While liquidated damages clauses are generally allowed, a court may strike one down if the clause appears to be proxying for punitive damages or if the terms of the clause are unconscionable . [Last reviewed in June of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics wex articles business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure legal education and practice legal theory money and financial problems Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will return' to 'will consider returning' introduces ambiguity regarding the Recipient's obligation to return confidential information, as described in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation points out that Section 235 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts requires full performance of a contractual duty. The modified clause creates room for the Recipient not to fulfill their obligation, potentially leading to a breach of contract. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states, 'A breach of contract occurs whenever a party who entered a contract fails to perform their promised obligations.' The modified text weakens the original obligation, creating a contradiction by allowing the Recipient to potentially not return confidential information, thus failing to perform a promised obligation, as now it is discretionary and not mandatory.

perturbation.original_text: The undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 will continue in force [indefinitely][for [insert number] years from the date of this Agreement].
perturbation.changed_text: The undertakings in clauses 3 and 4 will generally continue unless circumstances dictate otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing a specific timeframe with 'generally continue unless circumstances dictate otherwise' introduces significant ambiguity. The lack of defined circumstances creates a loophole that allows the Recipient to terminate the obligations prematurely.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Limitation Act 1980 establishes a statutory limitation period for bringing actions on simple contracts (typically six years). While NDAs can specify longer periods, the phrase 'unless circumstances dictate otherwise' introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent or waive these statutory limitations without clear and explicit terms, potentially rendering the enforceability of the NDA uncertain after the standard six-year period.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Limitation Act 1980 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia United Kingdom legislation Limitation Act 1980 Act of Parliament Parliament of the United Kingdom Long title An Act to consolidate the Limitation Acts 1939 to 1980. Citation 1980 c. 58 Dates Royal assent 13 November 1980 Commencement 1 May 1981 Other legislation Repeals/revokes Limitation Act 1939 Limitation Act 1975 Status: Amended Text of statute as originally enacted Revised text of statute as amended The Limitation Act 1980 (c. 58) [ 1 ] is an act of the Parliament of the United Kingdom applicable only to England and Wales. It is a statute of limitations which provides timescales within which action may be taken (by issuing a claim form) for breaches of the law. For example, it provides that breaches of an ordinary contract are actionable for six years after the event [ 2 ] whereas breaches of a deed are actionable for twelve years after the event. [ 3 ] In most cases, after the expiry of the time periods specified in the act the remedies available for breaches are extinguished and no action may be taken in the courts in respect of those breaches. Summary of time limits [ edit ] The ordinary time limits allowed by the act are set out below. These limits may, in some cases, be extended or altered. Most of the time limits run from the day after the accrual of action, which is "the earliest time at which an action could be brought". [ 4 ] If the potential claimant was not at least 18 or did not have a sound mind at the time of the accrual of action, time will not run until he is at least 18 and has sound mind. [ 5 ] Where there has been fraud or concealment, or the action is for relief from the consequences of a mistake, time will not run until the fraud, concealment or mistake is discovered or could with reasonable diligence be discovered. [ 6 ] Ordinarily applicable limitation periods for common types of claim Type of claim General limitation period Trusts Fraudulent breach of trust No limit [ 7 ]
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces a specific timeframe for undertakings with the ambiguous phrase "generally continue unless circumstances dictate otherwise." This introduces significant uncertainty regarding the duration of the obligations. The Limitation Act 1980, as referenced in both scraped snippets and the law explanation, establishes time limits for bringing actions on contracts (e.g., six years for simple contracts). While the original text allowed for a defined indefinite or multi-year duration, the modified text's ambiguous wording could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent these statutory limitations, potentially impacting the enforceability of the agreement. The phrase introduces a loophole where the obligations could be prematurely terminated based on undefined 'circumstances,' contradicting the intent and potentially the enforceability within the statutory period outlined by the Limitation Act.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by, and is to be construed in accordance with, English law. The English Courts will have non-exclusive jurisdiction to deal with any dispute which has arisen or may arise out of, or in connection with, this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be interpreted in light of general legal principles. Disputes will be addressed through mutually agreed-upon channels.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'English law' with 'general legal principles' removes the specificity of the governing law, making it unclear which legal framework applies. Replacing 'English Courts will have non-exclusive jurisdiction' with 'Disputes will be addressed through mutually agreed-upon channels' eliminates the default legal venue and replaces it with an undefined process, weakening enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Rome I Regulation governs contractual obligations within the EU (and still influences English Law post-Brexit). While parties have the freedom to choose the governing law, Article 3 requires that choice to be express or clearly demonstrated by the terms of the contract. The modified text, by removing the express choice of English law and replacing it with 'general legal principles,' creates ambiguity and uncertainty. This contradicts the requirement for a clear choice of law, making it difficult to determine which jurisdiction's laws should govern the interpretation and enforcement of the agreement.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies English law as the governing law and English courts as the jurisdiction for disputes. The changed text replaces this with 'general legal principles' and 'mutually agreed-upon channels' for dispute resolution. The Law Explanation points out that Article 3 of the Rome I Regulation requires a clear choice of law. The modified text, by removing the express choice of English law, creates ambiguity. Even without relevant scraped law snippets, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, combined with the original and changed text, demonstrate that the change introduces ambiguity about which jurisdiction's laws should govern the agreement, contradicting the need for a clear choice of law, and meets the definition of 'Ambiguity Legal Contradiction'.
